coredata.dc:title: Preparation of twin graphene quantum dots through the electric-field-assisted femtosecond laser ablation of graphene dispersions

coredata.dc:description: Twin graphene can introduce stable and extended defects to energy band engineering, giving unique electron transport properties, which is expected to have potential applications in the fields of magnetism, spin transport or photoluminescence. This study proposes a method of using electric-field-assisted temporally-shaped femtosecond laser ablation liquid (ETLAL) of graphene dispersion to prepare graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with an average particle size of 2–3 nm and oxygen-containing functional groups modified surface, which realizes the controllable preparation of single crystal and twin GQDs (5-fold twin). This method controls the crystallinity of GQDs from two aspects: (1) The intervention of an electric field can rapidly command the directional motion of the cavitation bubbles and the nanoparticles contained therein to collide and crystallize at higher temperatures and pressures, which is the key to the formation of twin GQD; (2) Adjusting temporally-shaped femtosecond laser pulse delay could control the proportion of the Coulomb explosion during the ablation process, which increases the carbon cluster supplied by the cavitation bubble which is the key of polyploid number in twin GQDs. This research provides a fast, green strategy for the preparation of unprecedented twin GQDs, which is of great significance to the applications in the field of 2D material defect engineering.

Plasma evolution under laser ablation liquid under different reaction conditions (a) femtosecond laser ablation liquid (LAL), (b) electric-field-assisted femtosecond laser ablation liquid (ELAL), and (c) electric-field-assisted temporally shaped femtosecond laser ablation liquid (ETLAL). The time range is 50–80 ns, and each result in the picture is obtained at 10 ns intervals. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Morphological characterization of GQDs. (a, b) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) images of single-pulse femtosecond laser-graphene quantum dots (S-GQDs); (c, d) TEM and AFM images of electric-field-assisted single-pulse femtosecond laser-graphene quantum dots (E-GQDs); (e, f) TEM and AFM images of electric-field-assisted temporally shaped femtosecond laser-graphene quantum dots (ET-GQDs). Inset images in (a, c, e) are high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of GQDs crystals. Inset images in (b, d, f) plot the height profiles corresponding with the lines in (b, d, f). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Morphological characterization of electric-field-assisted temporally shaped femtosecond laser-graphene quantum dots (ET-GQDs). (a, b) HRTEM images of ET-GQDs (scale bar: 10 nm); (c) partial enlarged view of the area marked in (a) (scale bar: 2 nm); (d) schematic of the structure of ET-GQDs of (c); (e, f) fast Fourier transform results of the structure in the marked areas (1, 4) in (c). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Characterization of the surface state of GQDs. (a, b) ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrum, excitation spectrum, and x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) image of S-GQDs; (c, d) UV–vis spectrum, excitation spectrum, and XPS image of electric-field-assisted E-GQDs; (e, f) UV–vis spectrum, excitation spectrum, and XPS spectrum of ET-GQDs. head.ce:title: Preparation of twin graphene quantum dots through the electric-field-assisted femtosecond laser ablation of graphene dispersions

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Twin graphene can introduce stable and extended defects to energy band engineering, giving unique electron transport properties, which is expected to have potential applications in the fields of magnetism, spin transport or photoluminescence. This study proposes a method of using electric-field-assisted temporally-shaped femtosecond laser ablation liquid (ETLAL) of graphene dispersion to prepare graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with an average particle size of 2–3 nm and oxygen-containing functional groups modified surface, which realizes the controllable preparation of single crystal and twin GQDs (5-fold twin). This method controls the crystallinity of GQDs from two aspects: (1) The intervention of an electric field can rapidly command the directional motion of the cavitation bubbles and the nanoparticles contained therein to collide and crystallize at higher temperatures and pressures, which is the key to the formation of twin GQD; (2) Adjusting temporally-shaped femtosecond laser pulse delay could control the proportion of the Coulomb explosion during the ablation process, which increases the carbon cluster supplied by the cavitation bubble which is the key of polyploid number in twin GQDs. This research provides a fast, green strategy for the preparation of unprecedented twin GQDs, which is of great significance to the applications in the field of 2D material defect engineering.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the unique quantum effects of quantum dots (QDs), their potential in biology, optoelectronics, and energy-related applications has attracted tremendous attention. They have become among the fastest growing fields and most exciting interfaces of nanotechnology [1,2]. CQDs, especially the graphene quantum dots (GQDs), have been used in various fields [3], such as bioimaging and light-emitting devices [4], because of their excellent biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, broadband optical absorption, stable photoluminescence (PL) [5], and resistance to photo bleaching [6,7]. The approaches for fabricating GQDs involve two main strategies: (1) Top-down, which use hydrothermal cutting [8–11], strong acid-assisted cracking [12,13], laser liquid ablation [14], ultrasonic fragmentation [15], electrochemical [16,17], and electro-Fenton reaction [18,19] to break down carbon sources; (2) bottom-up, which use hydrothermal [20,21] or microwave-assisted hydrothermal treatment [22] to carbonize organic molecules or polymers for carbonization synthesis and surface functionalization. Different methods could prepare GQDs with different sizes [23–25], morphologies, hydrophilic oxidizing groups [26,27], doping elements [28–30], and defects [31–33], which produces band gaps and make the GQDs PL [34].

Numerical evidence twinning acts as accommodation/relaxation mechanisms in graphene [35], the abnormal transmission characteristics of the Massless Dirac fermions in graphene are directly related to the average grain size and the density of non-hexagonal defects [36]. Unlike vacancies, it does not perturb the electronic structure locally and can induce band gap of 1.5 eV [37], which is higher than the stress (0.4 eV) [38] and doping elements (0.67 eV) [39], so twinning is one of known regulations of graphene [40], which is expected to have potential applications in the fields of magnetism, spin transport or photoluminescence. The formation mechanism [41–43] and electron transport properties [44,45] of large-area polycrystalline and grain-boundary graphene have been theoretically calculated, but no observations on the nucleation mechanism and optical properties of twin GQDs have been reported. Moreover, band gap manipulation and controlling the density of grain boundaries (GBs) in crystallization of GQDs are still challenges. The classic crystal growth theory of graphene uphold that the nucleation density and growth rate depend on temperature and carbon (C) supply in a non-linear manner [44]. Then, adjusting the carbon density and crystallization environment in the crystallization process of GQDs will be an effective means to control the crystallization of graphene.

Compared with the aforementioned methods, laser ablation in liquid (LAL) is environmentally friendly and can be used to obtain metastable phases nanoparticle that may be unavailable in normal environments [46]. The complex physicochemical processes that occur during the LAL process can be divided into three phases: the plasma phase, the cavitation bubble phase, and the chemical modification phase [47]. Different physical processes are involved when the laser with different parameters interact with the material: The millisecond (ms) LAL mechanism is primarily a heating effect [48]; The nanosecond (ns) and picosecond (ps) LAL are similar in that they are both photothermal mechanisms [49–51]; The femtosecond laser(fs) LAL may involve processes such as phase explosion, fragmentation, and Coulomb explosion [52]. Femtosecond lasers offer a clear advantage in inducing Coulomb explosions because their pulse durations are shorter than the electron–phonon coupling time of graphene nanosheets [53,54]. Coulomb explosions are the fundamental cause of nanocrystal fragmentation, which is an important source of material for the formation of new nanostructures in the LAL process [55,56]. In addition, changing the external environment of the LAL process by means of electrochemistry [57,58], magnetic [59], and temperature fields [60] will also have effect on the morphological and size of the generated nanoparticles. Thus, additional field-assisted femtosecond laser liquid-phase processing may provide new opportunities for the fabrication of controlled nanostructures.

In this study, we used an electric-field-assisted temporally-shaped femtosecond laser ablation liquid (ETLAL) of graphene dispersion. By adjusting the temporally shaped fs laser pulse delay and the electric field intensity, we obtained a series of GQDs that were similar in size but different in crystallinity and oxygen degrees. The proposed method has the following major advantages over current strategies: (1) The experimental process is straightforward and clean, preventing the formation of atomic impurities and ensuring the purity of the GQDs; (2) According to the classical crystal growth theory of graphene, the nucleation density and growth rate depend on temperature and C supply in a non-linear manner. The intervention of the electric field can quickly guide the directional movement of the cavitation bubbles and the nanoparticles contained in them. The collision of the crystal nuclei can be completed under higher temperature and pressure conditions, and then the structure of polyploidy twin GQDs can be formed. Adjusting the temporally shaped femtosecond laser pulse delay during the ablation process could control the proportion of the Coulomb explosion which could optimize the concentration of small graphene fragments and C clusters in the cavitation bubbles, which is the polyploidy twin GQDs (such as 5-fold twin GQDs) nucleated C supply. (3) Both the femtosecond laser and the electric field can ionize the solution to generate free oxygen-containing functional groups, which can modify the unsaturated carbon atoms on the edges and the GBs of twin GQDs to realize the laser-dependent excitation fluorescence effect.

Prepare graphene dispersion: 10 mg graphene nanosheets was dispersed into 10 mL mixture of distilled water and ethanol via ultrasonicatio (20 min), and the ratio of distilled water and ethanol is 1:1. The graphene nanosheets (diameter: 1–3 μm; thickness: 1–5 nm) was purchased from XFNANO, Inc. (Nanjing, China). The morphology and properties of graphene nanosheets in the prepared graphene dispersion are shown in Fig. S1.

Synthesis of GQDs: The Monolayer or bilayer GQDs with controllable defects, uniformity, and a small size (average particle size of 2–3 nm) were synthesized through ETLAL of graphene dispersion. A schematic illustration of the experimental setup is shown in Fig. 1 a, femtosecond laser beam was focused into graphene dispersion (total laser fluence 1.112 J cm−2) by 150 mm cylindrical lens for 50 min. When the incident spot diameter was fixed at about 6 mm, the processing regions are estimated to be about 1256 mm2, and short-term conduction is formed between the platinum electrodes on both sides of the cell. The computer controls the horizontal movement of the six-degree-of-freedom translation stage (Physik Instrumente, M-840.5DG) where the cell is located to achieve the relative motion of the femtosecond laser spot and the graphene dispersion, ensuring that the graphene nanosheets inside the cell are completely processed. Platinum foil electrodes are fixed at both ends of the reaction cell and connected to a DC power supplied to provide an experimental power with a voltage of 0–5 V. Herein, we use ETLAL to prepare a series of GQDs, and named according to the different parameters of the laser and electric field during the preparation process: The GQDs prepared with a conventional single-pulse femtosecond laser and an applied electric field of 0 V are named S-GQDs; The GQDs prepared with a conventional single-pulse femtosecond laser and an applied electric field of 5 V are named E-GQDs; The GQDs prepared with temporally-shaped femtosecond laser pulse delay of 10 ps and an applied electric field of 5 V are named ET-GQDs.

Monolayer or bilayer GQDs with controllable defects, uniformity, and a small size (average particle size of 2–3 nm) were synthesized through ETLAL of graphene dispersion. The process of using ETLAL of graphene dispersion to prepare GQDs can be divided into three stages similar to the LAL process: the plasma phase, the cavitation bubble phase, and the chemical modification phase.

The plasma phase consists of a complex physical process (Fig. 1b). Firstly, the graphene nanosheets suspended in garphene dispersions absorb the energy by the femtosecond laser pulse, then undergoes collisional ionization, electron heating and photoionization in femtosecond scale, followed by Coulomb explosion and photothermal vaporization in picosecond scale, and finally plasma generation, expansion and collapse in nanosecond scale [61]. At this stage, the C-clusters and primary particles in the plasma will be the main material in the subsequent cavitation bubbles and the only carbon source for the recrystallization of GQDs [44]. Considering that Coulomb explosion is a major factor in reducing the size of highly charged nanoparticles, controlling the dominance of Coulomb explosion in femtosecond laser ablation will allow to control the density of C clusters in the plasma. This would be an effective way to control the state of GQD crystals [62,63]. The pulse interval of conventional femtosecond laser singlepulse liquid phase ablation (frequency is 1 KHz)is as long as 1 ms, which is longer than the time scale of the three stages of LAL. Therefore, it is considered that the fragmentation and recrystallization process occurs after the material absorbs the laser pulse, which is not affected by the next laser pulse, so that the ucleation environment is relatively stable and the crystallinity well [64]. The temporally-shaped femtosecond laser pulse train divides a single pulse into two subpulses while keeping the total fluence constant. As the first subpulse radiates on the surface of a graphene nanosheet, the heated electrons overlap. Therefore, the interlayer interaction of graphene nanosheets is reduced by the Coulomb interference force. The second sub-pulse arrived 10 ps later than the first sub-pulse, which means the electrons and incident recombination on the surface of the graphene nanosheet is not completed at that time. So the ionization induction is enhanced, resulting in an enhanced Coulomb explosion of graphene nanosheets [65]. Thus, the temporally shaped femtosecond laser can induce and control the Coulomb explosion, which in turn regulates the C-cluster density in the plasma and provides the material basis for twin GQDs nucleation. Another factor of nucleation of GQDs, temperature, is not considered here because the ablation temperature does not change much and the laser energy density is constant when the material is being processed.

We used laser-induced plasma (LIP) to record the plasma strength of electric-field-assisted temporally-shaped femtosecond laser ablation liquid, and revealed the influence of assisted electric field and temporally-shaped femtosecond laser on plasma evolution. In view of the whole volume, plasma is positively related to the size of the laser spot and the laser flux. In order to facilitate observation, we used a plano-convex lens that replaced the cylindrical mirror to reduce the diameter of the plasma. We used the laser power (25 mW) to excite the plasma and used an ICCD to capture images. As shown in Fig. 2 , we obtained pictures of plasma evolution according to the different parameters of the laser and electric field during the preparation process. The image captured time range is 50–80 ns, and the interval is 10 ns. The brightness of the plasma evolution in the image is positively correlated with the plasma intensity, of which the maximum value was 53066 counts (Fig. 2a). The plasma generated in the LAL process was subjected to the pressure from the surrounding liquid, showing a relatively regular circle. In the ELAL process, the volume of the plasma became significantly larger and the intensity increased (Fig. 2b) horizontally to the direction of the electric field, since the electron concentration at the plasma nucleus can reach 1021. The electric field applied in the experiment could only act on the high-energy particles at the edge of the plasma to attract or repel the movement. So the size of the plasma increased slightly in the horizontal direction. In the vertical direction, the high-energy particles diffuse to the surroundings with a higher initial acceleration under the action of the electric field, because there is no attraction or repulsion of the external electric field in the vertical direction. As a result, the kinetic energy of the high-energy particles only opposes the lower liquid surface pressure, so there is a large increase in height, forming an elliptical plasma. The maximum background brightness value could be 64002 counts, which was produced in the ETLAL process. The morphology of plasma is similar to that produced in the ELAL process, and both have a relatively obvious tail in height. However, because the sub-pulse of the time-shaping femtosecond laser only has half of the total energy, the plasma volume is smaller, with the maximum background brightness value of 34020 counts. As shown in the marked area in Fig. 2c (the red line), it should be a part of the plasma generated in the first sub-pulse. The death rate of plasma shows that when an electric field is applied, the death rate of plasma is significantly faster than that in the LAL environment. It is also confirmed that the electric-field can guide the movement of high-energy particles, thereby accelerating the temperature exchange between the plasma and the liquid environment.

During plasma decay, energy is transferred to the liquid and a vapor layer is formed in the liquid that is roughly the same volume as the plasma, and the vapor layer grows into a cavitation bubble as the plasma is extinguished (Fig. 1c) [65,66]. Each cavitation bubble forms a clear spherical boundary within microseconds after a brief period of coexistence with the plasma [56,61]. The spherical bubble lasts for a few hundred microseconds. The C atom clusters and primary particles are likely to be trapped inside the cavitation bubbles, where favorable pressure and temperature conditions for nanoparticle nucleation, growth, coalescence, and solidification are realized [67]. The incubation atmosphere provided by cavitation bubbles is generally considered equivalent to the chemical vapor deposition (CVD) growth environment of graphene, and the pressure and temperature of the cavitation bubbles affect the motion trajectory and collision probability of the C clusters and primary particles, thereby affecting the performance of graphene crystallization [62,68,69]. Because the cavitation bubbles produced during LAL are electrically conductive and have free charge (this characteristic was generated by ultrasound and was not observed), the external electric field will have a directional guiding effect on the cavitation bubbles and high-energy nanoparticles [70]. Compared with the natural collision crystallization of nanoparticles, under the influence of an external electric field, C atom clusters and the primary particles will vocally collide under unbalanced conditions, which greatly increases the probability of lattice mismatch, thereby generating GQDs with lattice defects.

Unlike nanoparticle crystallization and composition, which mainly occur inside the cavitation bubble, the chemical modification of as-synthesized nanoparticles may occur during each phase of electric-field-assisted femtosecond laser ablation liquid (ELAL), namely during plasma cooling, bubble expansion, or after bubble collapse. However, the particles that disperse and interact with liquid molecules after bubble collapse are the final chemical modifications of the recrystallized nanoparticles in the cavitation bubble. These nanoparticles can achieve a stable surface charge. The functional groups that modify the nanoparticles have two sources: the ionization of the solution mainly produced in the plasma phase by the femtosecond laser and the ionization of the solution by the external electric field that continues to be produced during the expansion and collapse of the cavitation bubble [71]. This is a crucial factor affecting the excitation-dependent PL performance of GQDs. The electron spin resonance (ESR)spectroscopy reveals the types of free radicals produced during laser liquid phase ablation spin trap 5,5-dimethylpyrroline N-oxide (DMPO), which was used to capture nucleophilic hydroxyl radicals (-OH). Fig. S3. shows that after the introduction of DMPO, no obvious spectral lines were observed in the original graphene dispersion, but a typical DMPO/OH four-line spectrum with intensity of 1:2:2:1 is observed in the GQDs solution that produced after laser irradiation (Fig. S3). Obviously, the ESR spectroscopy shows that hydroxyl radicals are generated during the LAL process. At the same time, there are no other channels for obtaining –OH in the reaction system. Therefore, we confirm that the free radicals generated from the solution ionization by the femtosecond laser. They are the main source of the oxygen-containing functional groups modified on the surface of the experimentally obtained GQDs.

As the conventional LAL method, single-pulse femtosecond laser ablation is used to ablate graphene dispersion without an external electric field, which could obtain the S-GQDs with adequate crystallinity. Fig. 3 a shows a typical low-magnification transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100) image of the S-GQDs in which a relatively narrow size distribution of approximately 1–6 nm and an average lateral dimension of approximately 3.4 nm were observed(Fig. S4. a). Parallel and ordered lattice fringes in the as-prepared S-GQDs indicating that they are adequately crystallized, and the d-spacing of the S-GQDs was 0.21 nm which was assigned to the (100) face of the GQD crystals [72–74], as shown in the inset of Fig. 3a, an high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEM-2100F) image. Morphology and thickness of the S-GQDs were performed to record by an atomic force microscopy (AFM, Bruker Multimode 8) (Fig. 3b), and the corresponding height profile was presented in the inset of Fig. 3b. The thickness of most of the S-GQDs was <2 nm, indicating that most of the S-GQDs were monolayered or bilayered, which is consistent with related reports.

Compared with S-GQDs, the E-GQDs, which were obtained by ablating the graphene dispersion with an auxiliary single-pulse femtosecond laser, were similar in size and morphology, but significant difference in the lattice structure was observed. Fig. 3c reveals that the E-GQDs were homogeneously dispersed and exhibited a relatively narrow size distribution of approximately 1–5 nm and an average lateral dimension of approximately 2.7 nm (Fig. S4b). AFM measurements were also performed to confirm the morphology and thickness of the E-GQDs (Fig. 3d). Most of the E-GQDs were <2 nm thick, indicating that most of them were monolayered or bilayered. The interlunar spacing of many nanocrystals was approximately 0.21 nm, which is consistent with the (100) lattice fringe D value of graphene. The HRTEM image in the inset of Fig. 3c clearly indicates a nanocrystal with 2-fold twin GQDs, the structure of which has rarely been reported in research on conventional LAL. This result indicates that GQDs with an enhanced crystal structure can be obtained using the traditional LAL method without an electrical field, but the twin GQDs can only be obtained through the use of an electric field, which could guide the collision of cavitation bubbles and their internal atomic clusters in extreme environments. Therefore, the application of an electrical field during the femtosecond laser ablation liquid of graphene dispersion plays a crucial role in the formation of twin GQDs. On the basis of the aforementioned results, we further explored the effects of graphene nanosheet fragmentation on the formation of twin GQDs. By temporally shaping a conventional single pulse into a two-subpulse train, we improved the production rate of GQD nanomaterials and the proportion of uniform, small GQDs. This finding corroborates previous results that a temporally shaped femtosecond laser two-subpulse train promotes the nonthermal phase transition mechanism (Coulomb repulsion) to dominate the ablation process when a two-dimensional (2D) QD material is prepared, thereby producing nanoparticles with a smaller size, narrower distribution, and a more adequate QD crystal structure. Therefore, the laser parameters are crucial factors that influence the generation of nanoparticles and atomic clusters in the plasma phase. Unfortunately, they have little effect on nanoparticle recrystallization during cavitation bubble evolution. We applied an external electric field during the temporally shaped femtosecond laser processing to obtain ET-GQDs, which were homogeneously dispersed (Fig. 3e) and had a thickness of <2 nm (Fig. 3f). The size distribution of the ET-GQDs was narrow, and most of particles were <2.5 nm in size (Fig. S3c). However, the HRTEM image in the inset of Fig. 3e clearly depicts 5-fold twins in the ET-GQD nanocrystals, which were much more polycrystalline than the ET-GQDs were.

Another HRTEM analysis revealed the local assembly state of the twining and monodispersed crystalline GQDs mixed in the sample (Fig. 4 a and b); the red frames mark GQDs with twinned. In addition, Fig. 4c is a high-resolution image of a nanocrystal with 5-fold twin GQDs, in which the GBs are indicated by yellow dashed lines, and each twin variant is marked with a number (1–5). A schematic of the 5-fold GQDs is presented in Fig. 4d. The green rings on the grain boundary indicate the standard hexagonal ring structure of graphene, the yellow rings are heptagonal, and the red rings are pentagonal. The heptagonal ring structure appeared along with the pentagonal ring structure. this result is consistent with those of previous studies [75–77]. The corresponding 2D fast Fourier transform (FFT) patterns (Fig. 4e and f) were used to identify the orientation of ET-GQDs marked in areas 1 and 4 in Fig. 4c. The FFT patterns in different areas indicate that the orientation of graphene crystals in the ET-GQDs was single and uniform and that the angles between them were consistent with the angles of their lattices. The analysis of the HRTEM images revealed that twin GQDs accounted for a large proportion of the ET-GQDs, and this structure was not produced by the folding of multilayered graphene.

The aforementioned results prove our assumption that twin GQDs can be controllable synthesized through ETLAL of graphene dispersion. Laser parameters, particularly femtosecond laser shaping pulses, can be used to adjust the Coulomb explosion during the interaction between laser and graphene dispersion to subsequently produce cavitation bubbles containing more and smaller graphene particles and atomic clusters, which are the material sources of the formed GQDs. Subsequently, an external electric field that increases the temperature and pressure can be used to add the collision probability of the point particles in the cavitation bubbles, forming twin GQDs. At the same time, we can draw a general conclusion that when GQDs are prepared by ETLAL, the reaction parameters that affect the GQD granularity and the number of layers include the temporally-shaped femtosecond laser pulse stepwise, while the main parameter that affects the crystal state of GQDs is the electric field.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optical properties of twin GQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
When the femtosecond laser is focused on water, laser ionization and the photodissociation of water molecules form –H+, –O2−, and –OH− radicals, as indicated in reaction (1). and the subsequent reactions form H2 and O2 as well as H2O2 [77], and observably, indicating that abundant –OH was produced from the catalytic decomposition of H2O2. Since the solvent used in this experiment is a mixture of water and ethanol, H2O2 will act as a strong oxidant to react with ethanol, as indicated in reaction (3).

The above reaction generates –O2-,–OH- and –COOH- in the solution, which is the source of the oxygen-containing functional groups modified on the surface of GQDs [78].

In most situations, the PL wavelength and intensity of GQDs is relative to the number of surface groups rather than size. In order to qualitatively and quantitatively investigate the composition, chemical bonding and associate functional groups of GQDs samples, we carried out ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrum, excitation spectrum, x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy(XPS) and FTIR spectroscopy. The FTIR spectrum shown in Fig. S5 reveals that all GQDs exhibit tensile vibration of the –OH bond at 3420 cm−1. However, compared with S-GQDs, in the FTIR spectra of E-GQDs and ET-GQDs, the vibrations of CO double bonds(1630 cm−1) and C–O bonds(1045 cm−1) are observed, which are consistent with the oxygen-containing functional groups generated by the above reaction.

Fig. 5 a reveals the UV–vis absorption spectrum and excitation spectrum of S-GQDs synthesized using LAL. The typical feature at approximately 230 nm is attributed to the π–π∗ transition of aromatic sp2 domains, and the absorption shoulders at approximately 275 nm can be attributed to the n–π∗ transition. The PL maximum emission wavelength fixed at 440 nm and was independent of the increase in excitation wavelength (330–370 nm), which could be explained the uniform sizes and the surface states of the sp2 clusters contained in S-GQDs [2]. However, in the UV–vis absorption spectrum and excitation spectrum of the twin E-GQDs and ET-GQDs (Fig. 5c and d), the absorption shoulders at approximately 275 nm are attributable to the n–π∗ transition, and the intensity increases with the number of twin GQDs. UV–vis spectrum was used to characterize the optical absorption of the experimentally prepared GQDs, and to estimate the optical absorption cut-off edge of the GQDs. Based on the Kubelka–Munk function and the absorption spectrum, calculate the relationship between
( A h v / 2 )
and wavelength as a function of wavelength, where A is the absorption coefficient, and the linear data segment is linearly fitted by extension to estimate the energy of the experimentally obtained GQDs band gap. The band gap of the S-GQDs, the E-GQDs and the ET-GQDs is 3.86 eV, 3.92 eV and 3.943 eV, respectively (Fig. S6). In addition, the emission wavelength of PL results in red shifts as the excitation wavelength increases (330–370 nm). The PL properties of the ET- GQDs exhibit excitation-dependent behavior obviously, which is positively related to the number of groups. The C1s XPS survey spectra of the three GQDs (Fig. S7) only have 285 eV and 533 eV doublets, which represent the carbon and oxygen elements in GQDs, respectively. With the increase of twin GQDs, the intensity of the O1s peak gradually increases, which means that the twin structure can increase the oxygen loading of GQDs. In the XPS spectrum of the GQD data (Fig. 5b, d, f), the peaks at 284.5 eV are attributed to sp2 carbon (CC), and those at 286.4 (CO groups), 288.4 eV (OC–OH groups) are attributed to sp3 carbon clusters. Some studies believe that GO and GQD have similar chemical structures, and GO contains oxygen-based functional groups on the base or edge [79]. Therefore, in such sp2 clusters, the 2–3 nm aromatic sp2 linearly arranged epoxy and hydroxyl-bonded sp3 domains are surrounded by C–O (e–h) pairs [26]. These functional groups can promote fluorescence. XPS spectroscopy confirmed that the GQD obtained by our experiment contains a mixture of sp2 clusters and some conjugated repeating units dispersed in an insulating sp3 matrix. The percentages of S-GQD, E-GQD and ET-GQD estimated from XPS spectra for carbon groups are listed in Table S1, and the relative fractions of sp2-bonded carbon contents are 49.63%, 37.82% and 31.11%, respectively. With the increase of twin crystal structure, the sp3 carbon bond ratio of oxygen-containing functional groups in GQDs increases significantly, which indicates that more oxygen-containing functional groups will be carried on in twin GQDs. Since the oxygen-containing functional groups on GQDs only come from the ionization of the solvent by the femtosecond laser, according to the radical trapping results in Fig. S3. It can be seen that twin GQDs will carry more hydroxyl radicals. Therefore, when the excitation wavelength changes from 310 nm to 370 nm, the emission redshift, which may be caused by the abundant carboxyl groups in the periphery of GQD. The peripheral carboxyl groups passivate the GQD, which may lead to high quantum yield and good stability [11]. Therefore, the quantum yield of GQD also increases with the increase of hydroxyl group. The quantum yield of S-GQDs is 12.13%; the quantum yield of E-GQD is 18.48%; the quantum yield of ET-GQDs is 26.75%.

To explore carrier dynamics in twin GQDs, we monitored the fluorescence lifetime of the series of GQDs prepared in our experiment. As shown in Fig. 6 , the PL dynamics of GQDs detected near the fluorescence peak were monitored with an excitation wavelength of 344 nm (NanoLOG-TCSPC) at room temperature. All fluorescence transients were fitted with biexponential function well,

) are the summation values of the percentage contributions to the faster (slower) decay, which means that the PL dynamics exhibit two distinct relaxation timescales. Espectively, the best-fit parameters and average lifetimes are listed in Table S2. The biexponential fitting parameters(

) are (26.22%, 73.78%; 1.9420 ns, 9.0968 ns) for S-GQDs, (22.36%, 77.64%; 2.5056 ns, 9.6194 ns) for E-GQDs and (19.91%, 80.09%; 2.369 ns, 10.016 ns) for ET-GQDs, respectively.

As defects in the GQD cores increase (e.g., as twin GQDs emerge), both fast and slow components progressively lengthen and the average lifetimes increase with the emission wavelength. Generally, the fast decay guides the excited recombination of carriers on the surface state, and the slow decay releases the carriers from the green carbon core to the surface state [80]. Single-particle-based non-adiabatic molecular dynamics (NAMD) has been performed to simulate e-h recombination, demonstrating that the slow- and deep-level defects of monoatomic two-dimensional materials do not significantly accelerate the e-h recombination [81]. As a result, lifetimes are extended with increasing of twin GQDs, because of GQDs are mono-elemental materials, and do not form the unique impurity phonons that normally cause rapid e-h recombination. In addition, due to the flexibility of 2D materials, defects scatter phonons present in the original GQDs and they produce multiple modes at lower frequencies compared to the pristine GQDs and further inhibit e-h recombination.

We reported a method of preparing twin GQDs through the ETLAL of graphene dispersions. The controllable processing of twin GQDs was achieved by adjustment of the electric field and femtosecond laser parameters. The results demonstrated as follows: (1) The nanoparticles and atomic clusters produced by the Coulomb explosion in laser liquid phase ablation are the main sources of polycrystalline nanocrystal nuclei. Therefore, increasing the dominant position of the Coulomb explosion during laser ablation can increase the proportion of twin GQDs. (2) This method could quickly guide the directional movement of the cavitation bubbles and the nanoparticles contained within. Therefore the collision of crystal nuclei could be completed under higher temperature and pressure, which is the key to the formation of twin GQDs structure. (3) Together, the laser ablation and electric field ionization of the solution produce oxygen-containing functional groups, which can sufficiently modify unsaturated carbon atoms on the edges and GBs of twin GQDs. This study presented a rapid and green strategy for easily and efficiently preparing twin QDs for 2D materials in crystallization and defect engineering.
